question:
5. Prove that the simultaneous equations
\[
x^{4}-x^{2}=y^{4}-y^{2}=z^{4}-z^{2}
\]
are satisfied by the points of four straight lines and six ellipses, and by no other points.
solution:
Solution. Let \( L \) denote the locus of the given equations. Then a point is on \( L \) if and only if its coordinates ( \( x, y, z \) ) satisfy
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-1\right)\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(y^{2}+z^{2}-1\right)\left(y^{2}-z^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(z^{2}+x^{2}-1\right)\left(z^{2}-x^{2}\right)=0
\end{array}
\]

Consider the loci \( A, B, C, D \) defined as follows:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{l}
A:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
x^{2}+y^{2}-1 & =0 \\
y^{2}-z^{2} & =0,
\end{array}\right. \\
B:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
y^{2}+z^{2}-1 & =0 \\
z^{2}-x^{2} & =0
\end{array}\right. \\
C:\left\{\begin{array}{l}
z^{2}+x^{2}-1=0 \\
x^{2}-y^{2}
\end{array}=0,\right.
\end{array}\right] \begin{array}{l}
\text { D: } x^{2}=y^{2}=z^{2}
\end{array}
\]
\( A \) is the intersection of a right circular cylinder with the union of the planes \( z=y \) and \( z=-y \). Hence \( A \) is the union of two ellipses. Similarly \( B \) and \( C \) are each the union of two ellipses. \( D \), on the other hand, is the union of four straight lines, namely:
\[
x=y=z, \quad x=y=-z, \quad x=-y=z \quad \text { and } \quad x=-y=-z
\]

Any point common to \( A \) and \( B \) is in fact also on \( D \), so the ellipses of \( A \) and \( B \) are different. Similarly for \( B \) and \( C \) and for \( C \) and \( A \). Hence \( A \cup B \cup C \cup D \) consists of the union of 6 (distinct) ellipses and 4 (distinct) lines.

We now show that \( L=A \cup B \cup C \cup D \). If \( (x, y, z) \in A \), then evidently ( \( x, y, z \) ) satisfies (1), (2), and (3), the latter because
\[
z^{2}+x^{2}-1=\left(x^{2}+y^{2}-1\right)+\left(z^{2}-y^{2}\right)=0
\]

Thus \( A \subseteq L \). Similarly \( B \subseteq L \) and \( C \subseteq L \). It is immediate that \( D \subseteq L \). So \( A \cup B \cup C \cup D \subseteq L \).

Now consider a point \( p(x, y, z) \) of \( L \) that is not on \( D \). Assume, therefore, \( x^{2} \neq y^{2} \) and \( x^{2} \neq z^{2} \). Since \( p \) satisfies (1) and (3), we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
x^{2}+y^{2}-1=0 \\
x^{2}+z^{2}-1=0
\end{array}
\]
and therefore \( y^{2}=z^{2} \), so \( p \in A \). The other cases of inequalities lead to \( p \in B \) or \( p \in C \) by the same argument. Hence \( L \subseteq A \cup B \cup C \cup D \) and indeed \( L=A \cup B \cup C \cup D \) is the union of 4 lines and 6 ellipses.

descriptive_long.question:
5. Prove that the simultaneous equations
\[
absciss^{4}-absciss^{2}=ordinate^{4}-ordinate^{2}=applicate^{4}-applicate^{2}
\]
are satisfied by the points of four straight lines and six ellipses, and by no other points.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( locusmain \) denote the locus of the given equations. Then a point is on \( locusmain \) if and only if its coordinates ( \( absciss, ordinate, applicate \) ) satisfy
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(absciss^{2}+ordinate^{2}-1\right)\left(absciss^{2}-ordinate^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(ordinate^{2}+applicate^{2}-1\right)\left(ordinate^{2}-applicate^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(applicate^{2}+absciss^{2}-1\right)\left(applicate^{2}-absciss^{2}\right)=0
\end{array}
\]

Consider the loci \( locusalpha, locusbeta, locusgamma, locusdelta \) defined as follows:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{l}
 locusalpha:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
 absciss^{2}+ordinate^{2}-1 & =0 \\
 ordinate^{2}-applicate^{2} & =0,
\end{array}\right. \\
 locusbeta:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
 ordinate^{2}+applicate^{2}-1 & =0 \\
 applicate^{2}-absciss^{2} & =0
\end{array}\right. \\
 locusgamma:\left\{\begin{array}{l}
 applicate^{2}+absciss^{2}-1=0 \\
 absciss^{2}-ordinate^{2}
\end{array}=0,\right.
\end{array}\right] \begin{array}{l}
 \text { locusdelta: } absciss^{2}=ordinate^{2}=applicate^{2}
\end{array}
\]
\( locusalpha \) is the intersection of a right circular cylinder with the union of the planes \( applicate=ordinate \) and \( applicate=-ordinate \). Hence \( locusalpha \) is the union of two ellipses. Similarly \( locusbeta \) and \( locusgamma \) are each the union of two ellipses. \( locusdelta \), on the other hand, is the union of four straight lines, namely:
\[
absciss=ordinate=applicate, \quad absciss=ordinate=-applicate, \quad absciss=-ordinate=applicate \quad \text { and } \quad absciss=-ordinate=-applicate
\]

Any point common to \( locusalpha \) and \( locusbeta \) is in fact also on \( locusdelta \), so the ellipses of \( locusalpha \) and \( locusbeta \) are different. Similarly for \( locusbeta \) and \( locusgamma \) and for \( locusgamma \) and \( locusalpha \). Hence \( locusalpha \cup locusbeta \cup locusgamma \cup locusdelta \) consists of the union of 6 (distinct) ellipses and 4 (distinct) lines.

We now show that \( locusmain=locusalpha \cup locusbeta \cup locusgamma \cup locusdelta \). If \( (absciss, ordinate, applicate) \in locusalpha \), then evidently ( \( absciss, ordinate, applicate \) ) satisfies (1), (2), and (3), the latter because
\[
applicate^{2}+absciss^{2}-1=\left(absciss^{2}+ordinate^{2}-1\right)+\left(applicate^{2}-ordinate^{2}\right)=0
\]

Thus \( locusalpha \subseteq locusmain \). Similarly \( locusbeta \subseteq locusmain \) and \( locusgamma \subseteq locusmain \). It is immediate that \( locusdelta \subseteq locusmain \). So \( locusalpha \cup locusbeta \cup locusgamma \cup locusdelta \subseteq locusmain \).

Now consider a point \( p(absciss, ordinate, applicate) \) of \( locusmain \) that is not on \( locusdelta \). Assume, therefore, \( absciss^{2} \neq ordinate^{2} \) and \( absciss^{2} \neq applicate^{2} \). Since \( p \) satisfies (1) and (3), we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
absciss^{2}+ordinate^{2}-1=0 \\
absciss^{2}+applicate^{2}-1=0
\end{array}
\]
and therefore \( ordinate^{2}=applicate^{2} \), so \( p \in locusalpha \). The other cases of inequalities lead to \( p \in locusbeta \) or \( p \in locusgamma \) by the same argument. Hence \( locusmain \subseteq locusalpha \cup locusbeta \cup locusgamma \cup locusdelta \) and indeed \( locusmain=locusalpha \cup locusbeta \cup locusgamma \cup locusdelta \) is the union of 4 lines and 6 ellipses.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Prove that the simultaneous equations
\[
moonlight^{4}-moonlight^{2}=sunflower^{4}-sunflower^{2}=butterfly^{4}-butterfly^{2}
\]
are satisfied by the points of four straight lines and six ellipses, and by no other points.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( watercress \) denote the locus of the given equations. Then a point is on \( watercress \) if and only if its coordinates ( \( moonlight, sunflower, butterfly \) ) satisfy
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(moonlight^{2}+sunflower^{2}-1\right)\left(moonlight^{2}-sunflower^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(sunflower^{2}+butterfly^{2}-1\right)\left(sunflower^{2}-butterfly^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(butterfly^{2}+moonlight^{2}-1\right)\left(butterfly^{2}-moonlight^{2}\right)=0
\end{array}
\]

Consider the loci \( pineapple, strawberry, raspberry, blueberry \) defined as follows:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{l}
pineapple:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
moonlight^{2}+sunflower^{2}-1 & =0 \\
sunflower^{2}-butterfly^{2} & =0,
\end{array}\right. \\
strberry:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
sunflower^{2}+butterfly^{2}-1 & =0 \\
butterfly^{2}-moonlight^{2} & =0
\end{array}\right. \\
raspberry:\left\{\begin{array}{l}
butterfly^{2}+moonlight^{2}-1=0 \\
moonlight^{2}-sunflower^{2}
\end{array}=0,\right.
\end{array}\right] \begin{array}{l}
\text { blueberry: } moonlight^{2}=sunflower^{2}=butterfly^{2}
\end{array}
\]
\( pineapple \) is the intersection of a right circular cylinder with the union of the planes \( butterfly=sunflower \) and \( butterfly=-sunflower \). Hence \( pineapple \) is the union of two ellipses. Similarly \( strawberry \) and \( raspberry \) are each the union of two ellipses. \( blueberry \), on the other hand, is the union of four straight lines, namely:
\[
moonlight=sunflower=butterfly, \quad moonlight=sunflower=-butterfly, \quad moonlight=-sunflower=butterfly \quad \text { and } \quad moonlight=-sunflower=-butterfly
\]

Any point common to \( pineapple \) and \( strawberry \) is in fact also on \( blueberry \), so the ellipses of \( pineapple \) and \( strawberry \) are different. Similarly for \( strawberry \) and \( raspberry \) and for \( raspberry \) and \( pineapple \). Hence \( pineapple \cup strawberry \cup raspberry \cup blueberry \) consists of the union of 6 (distinct) ellipses and 4 (distinct) lines.

We now show that \( watercress=pineapple \cup strawberry \cup raspberry \cup blueberry \). If \( (moonlight, sunflower, butterfly) \in pineapple \), then evidently ( \( moonlight, sunflower, butterfly \) ) satisfies (1), (2), and (3), the latter because
\[
butterfly^{2}+moonlight^{2}-1=\left(moonlight^{2}+sunflower^{2}-1\right)+\left(butterfly^{2}-sunflower^{2}\right)=0
\]

Thus \( pineapple \subseteq watercress \). Similarly \( strawberry \subseteq watercress \) and \( raspberry \subseteq watercress \). It is immediate that \( blueberry \subseteq watercress \). So \( pineapple \cup strawberry \cup raspberry \cup blueberry \subseteq watercress \).

Now consider a point \( p(moonlight, sunflower, butterfly) \) of \( watercress \) that is not on \( blueberry \). Assume, therefore, \( moonlight^{2} \neq sunflower^{2} \) and \( moonlight^{2} \neq butterfly^{2} \). Since \( p \) satisfies (1) and (3), we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
moonlight^{2}+sunflower^{2}-1=0 \\
moonlight^{2}+butterfly^{2}-1=0
\end{array}
\]
and therefore \( sunflower^{2}=butterfly^{2} \), so \( p \in pineapple \). The other cases of inequalities lead to \( p \in strawberry \) or \( p \in raspberry \) by the same argument. Hence \( watercress \subseteq pineapple \cup strawberry \cup raspberry \cup blueberry \) and indeed \( watercress=pineapple \cup strawberry \cup raspberry \cup blueberry \) is the union of 4 lines and 6 ellipses.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. Prove that the simultaneous equations
\[
verticalaxis^{4}-verticalaxis^{2}=constantvalue^{4}-constantvalue^{2}=planaraxis^{4}-planaraxis^{2}
\]
are satisfied by the points of four straight lines and six ellipses, and by no other points.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( discretepoint \) denote the locus of the given equations. Then a point is on \( discretepoint \) if and only if its coordinates ( \( verticalaxis, constantvalue, planaraxis \) ) satisfy
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(verticalaxis^{2}+constantvalue^{2}-1\right)\left(verticalaxis^{2}-constantvalue^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(constantvalue^{2}+planaraxis^{2}-1\right)\left(constantvalue^{2}-planaraxis^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(planaraxis^{2}+verticalaxis^{2}-1\right)\left(planaraxis^{2}-verticalaxis^{2}\right)=0
\end{array}
\]

Consider the loci \( widearea, voidzone, solidfield, curvedpath \) defined as follows:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{l}
widearea:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
verticalaxis^{2}+constantvalue^{2}-1 & =0 \\
constantvalue^{2}-planaraxis^{2} & =0,
\end{array}\right. \\
voidzone:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
constantvalue^{2}+planaraxis^{2}-1 & =0 \\
planaraxis^{2}-verticalaxis^{2} & =0
\end{array}\right. \\
solidfield:\left\{\begin{array}{l}
planaraxis^{2}+verticalaxis^{2}-1=0 \\
verticalaxis^{2}-constantvalue^{2}
\end{array}=0,\right.
\end{array}\right] \begin{array}{l}
\text { curvedpath: } verticalaxis^{2}=constantvalue^{2}=planaraxis^{2}
\end{array}
\]
\( widearea \) is the intersection of a right circular cylinder with the union of the planes \( planaraxis=constantvalue \) and \( planaraxis=-constantvalue \). Hence \( widearea \) is the union of two ellipses. Similarly \( voidzone \) and \( solidfield \) are each the union of two ellipses. \( curvedpath \), on the other hand, is the union of four straight lines, namely:
\[
verticalaxis=constantvalue=planaraxis, \quad verticalaxis=constantvalue=-planaraxis, \quad verticalaxis=-constantvalue=planaraxis \quad \text { and } \quad verticalaxis=-constantvalue=-planaraxis
\]

Any point common to \( widearea \) and \( voidzone \) is in fact also on \( curvedpath \), so the ellipses of \( widearea \) and \( voidzone \) are different. Similarly for \( voidzone \) and \( solidfield \) and for \( solidfield \) and \( widearea \). Hence \( widearea \cup voidzone \cup solidfield \cup curvedpath \) consists of the union of 6 (distinct) ellipses and 4 (distinct) lines.

We now show that \( discretepoint=widearea \cup voidzone \cup solidfield \cup curvedpath \). If \( (verticalaxis, constantvalue, planaraxis) \in widearea \), then evidently ( \( verticalaxis, constantvalue, planaraxis \) ) satisfies (1), (2), and (3), the latter because
\[
planaraxis^{2}+verticalaxis^{2}-1=\left(verticalaxis^{2}+constantvalue^{2}-1\right)+\left(planaraxis^{2}-constantvalue^{2}\right)=0
\]

Thus \( widearea \subseteq discretepoint \). Similarly \( voidzone \subseteq discretepoint \) and \( solidfield \subseteq discretepoint \). It is immediate that \( curvedpath \subseteq discretepoint \). So \( widearea \cup voidzone \cup solidfield \cup curvedpath \subseteq discretepoint \).

Now consider a point \( p(verticalaxis, constantvalue, planaraxis) \) of \( discretepoint \) that is not on \( curvedpath \). Assume, therefore, \( verticalaxis^{2} \neq constantvalue^{2} \) and \( verticalaxis^{2} \neq planaraxis^{2} \). Since \( p \) satisfies (1) and (3), we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
verticalaxis^{2}+constantvalue^{2}-1=0 \\
verticalaxis^{2}+planaraxis^{2}-1=0
\end{array}
\]
and therefore \( constantvalue^{2}=planaraxis^{2} \), so \( p \in widearea \). The other cases of inequalities lead to \( p \in voidzone \) or \( p \in solidfield \) by the same argument. Hence \( discretepoint \subseteq widearea \cup voidzone \cup solidfield \cup curvedpath \) and indeed \( discretepoint=widearea \cup voidzone \cup solidfield \cup curvedpath \) is the union of 4 lines and 6 ellipses.

garbled_string.question:
Problem:
<<<
5. Prove that the simultaneous equations
\[
qzxwvtnp^{4}-qzxwvtnp^{2}=hjgrksla^{4}-hjgrksla^{2}=mdfplrqe^{4}-mdfplrqe^{2}
\]
are satisfied by the points of four straight lines and six ellipses, and by no other points.
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. Let \( asdfqwer \) denote the locus of the given equations. Then a point is on \( asdfqwer \) if and only if its coordinates ( \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mdfplrqe \) ) satisfy
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}-1\right)\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(hjgrksla^{2}+mdfplrqe^{2}-1\right)\left(hjgrksla^{2}-mdfplrqe^{2}\right)=0 \\
\left(mdfplrqe^{2}+qzxwvtnp^{2}-1\right)\left(mdfplrqe^{2}-qzxwvtnp^{2}\right)=0
\end{array}
\]

Consider the loci \( ksjdhfla, vbncxzpq, fghrtyui, lkjhgfas \) defined as follows:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{l}
ksjdhfla:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}-1 & =0 \\
hjgrksla^{2}-mdfplrqe^{2} & =0,
\end{array}\right. \\
vbncxzpq:\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
hjgrksla^{2}+mdfplrqe^{2}-1 & =0 \\
mdfplrqe^{2}-qzxwvtnp^{2} & =0
\end{array}\right. \\
fghrtyui:\left\{\begin{array}{l}
mdfplrqe^{2}+qzxwvtnp^{2}-1=0 \\
qzxwvtnp^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}
\end{array}=0,\right.
\end{array}\right] \begin{array}{l}
\text { lkjhgfas: } qzxwvtnp^{2}=hjgrksla^{2}=mdfplrqe^{2}
\end{array}
\]
\( ksjdhfla \) is the intersection of a right circular cylinder with the union of the planes \( mdfplrqe=hjgrksla \) and \( mdfplrqe=-hjgrksla \). Hence \( ksjdhfla \) is the union of two ellipses. Similarly \( vbncxzpq \) and \( fghrtyui \) are each the union of two ellipses. \( lkjhgfas \), on the other hand, is the union of four straight lines, namely:
\[
qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla=mdfplrqe, \quad qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla=-mdfplrqe, \quad qzxwvtnp=-hjgrksla=mdfplrqe \quad \text { and } \quad qzxwvtnp=-hjgrksla=-mdfplrqe
\]

Any point common to \( ksjdhfla \) and \( vbncxzpq \) is in fact also on \( lkjhgfas \), so the ellipses of \( ksjdhfla \) and \( vbncxzpq \) are different. Similarly for \( vbncxzpq \) and \( fghrtyui \) and for \( fghrtyui \) and \( ksjdhfla \). Hence \( ksjdhfla \cup vbncxzpq \cup fghrtyui \cup lkjhgfas \) consists of the union of 6 (distinct) ellipses and 4 (distinct) lines.

We now show that \( asdfqwer=ksjdhfla \cup vbncxzpq \cup fghrtyui \cup lkjhgfas \). If \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mdfplrqe) \in ksjdhfla \), then evidently ( \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mdfplrqe \) ) satisfies (1), (2), and (3), the latter because
\[
mdfplrqe^{2}+qzxwvtnp^{2}-1=\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}-1\right)+\left(mdfplrqe^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}\right)=0
\]

Thus \( ksjdhfla \subseteq asdfqwer \). Similarly \( vbncxzpq \subseteq asdfqwer \) and \( fghrtyui \subseteq asdfqwer \). It is immediate that \( lkjhgfas \subseteq asdfqwer \). So \( ksjdhfla \cup vbncxzpq \cup fghrtyui \cup lkjhgfas \subseteq asdfqwer \).

Now consider a point \( p(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mdfplrqe) \) of \( asdfqwer \) that is not on \( lkjhgfas \). Assume, therefore, \( qzxwvtnp^{2} \neq hjgrksla^{2} \) and \( qzxwvtnp^{2} \neq mdfplrqe^{2} \). Since \( p \) satisfies (1) and (3), we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}-1=0 \\
qzxwvtnp^{2}+mdfplrqe^{2}-1=0
\end{array}
\]
and therefore \( hjgrksla^{2}=mdfplrqe^{2} \), so \( p \in ksjdhfla \). The other cases of inequalities lead to \( p \in vbncxzpq \) or \( p \in fghrtyui \) by the same argument. Hence \( asdfqwer \subseteq ksjdhfla \cup vbncxzpq \cup fghrtyui \cup lkjhgfas \) and indeed \( asdfqwer=ksjdhfla \cup vbncxzpq \cup fghrtyui \cup lkjhgfas \) is the union of 4 lines and 6 ellipses.
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
Let

a(x)=x^{4}-2x^{2}, \qquad x\in\mathbb R.

In \(\mathbb R^{3}\) consider the locus

\[L=\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}:a(x)=a(y)=a(z)\}.\]

Show that L is the union of exactly four straight lines and six ellipses, and that no other points belong to L.  Give explicit equations for the four lines and the six ellipses.

(You should obtain the four lines
\[\ell_{1}:x=y=z,\;\;\ell_{2}:x=y=-z,\;\;\ell_{3}:x=-y=z,\;\;\ell_{4}:x=-y=-z,\]
and the six ellipses
\[
A_{\pm}:\begin{cases}y^{2}+z^{2}=2,\\ z=\pm x,\end{cases}\qquad
B_{\pm}:\begin{cases}z^{2}+x^{2}=2,\\ x=\pm y,\end{cases}\qquad
C_{\pm}:\begin{cases}x^{2}+y^{2}=2,\\ y=\pm z.\end{cases}
\])
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write a(t)=t^{4}-2t^{2}\;(t\in\mathbb R).  A point (x,y,z) is in L precisely when

\[a(x)=a(y)=a(z).\]

Factorisation of the pairwise differences.
For any real numbers u,v
\[
 a(u)-a(v)=u^{4}-v^{4}-2(u^{2}-v^{2})=(u^{2}-v^{2})(u^{2}+v^{2}-2).
\]
Hence the three equalities a(x)=a(y), a(y)=a(z), a(z)=a(x) are equivalent to
\[
\begin{cases}
(x^{2}-y^{2})(x^{2}+y^{2}-2)=0,\\
(y^{2}-z^{2})(y^{2}+z^{2}-2)=0,\\
(z^{2}-x^{2})(z^{2}+x^{2}-2)=0.
\end{cases}\tag{1}
\]
For every product at least one factor vanishes.  We distinguish cases.

1.  All three difference-factors vanish:  \(x^{2}=y^{2}=z^{2}.\)
Then x=\pm y and x=\pm z.  The eight sign possibilities collapse into four geometric lines (the common factor -1 yields the same set):
\[
\ell_{1}:x=y=z,\;\;\ell_{2}:x=y=-z,\;\;\ell_{3}:x=-y=z,\;\;\ell_{4}:x=-y=-z.\tag{2}
\]
Every point of the union \(\ell_{1}\cup\dots\cup\ell_{4}\) satisfies (1).

2.  At least one pair of squares is different.
Assume first
\[x^{2}\ne y^{2}.\]
Then for the first equation of (1) the factor \(x^{2}-y^{2}\) is non-zero, so the other factor must vanish:
\[x^{2}+y^{2}=2.\tag{3}\]

2a.  Suppose in addition \(y^{2}\ne z^{2}.\)
The second equation of (1) now yields \(y^{2}+z^{2}=2.\)  Subtracting this from (3) gives \(x^{2}=z^{2}\), i.e.  \(z=\pm x\).  Combining with (3) we obtain the two ellipses
\[
A_{\pm}:\;\begin{cases}y^{2}+z^{2}=2,\\ z=\pm x.\end{cases}
\]
They lie in the planes \(z=\pm x\) and are the sections of the circular cylinder \(y^{2}+z^{2}=2\) by those planes.

2b.  Suppose instead \(y^{2}=z^{2}.\)  (Because we are not on the lines (2) we still have \(x^{2}\ne z^{2}.\))  The second equation of (1) is automatically satisfied, while the third equation forces
\[z^{2}+x^{2}=2.\]
Together with \(y^{2}=z^{2}\) this gives
\[
 x^{2}+y^{2}=2,\qquad y=\pm z.
\]
Thus we obtain the two ellipses
\[
C_{\pm}:\;\begin{cases}x^{2}+y^{2}=2,\\ y=\pm z.\end{cases}
\]

Up to this point we have produced four of the six ellipses (A_{\pm}, C_{\pm}).

3.  Symmetric repetitions.
The defining equations (1) are completely symmetric in the variables x,y,z.  Cyclically permuting the roles of the coordinates in the analysis of Section 2 produces the remaining pair of ellipses
\[
B_{\pm}:\;\begin{cases}z^{2}+x^{2}=2,\\ x=\pm y,\end{cases}
\]
obtained by starting with the assumption \(y^{2}\ne z^{2}\) (or \(z^{2}\ne x^{2}\)).  Because the arguments are identical after the permutation, no new case-work is necessary.

4.  Exhaustion of the locus.
We have exhibited ten sets - the four lines (2) and the six ellipses A_{\pm},B_{\pm},C_{\pm}.  Every point on each of these ten sets satisfies (1), hence belongs to L:
\[
 \ell_{1}\cup\ell_{2}\cup\ell_{3}\cup\ell_{4}\cup A_{+}\cup A_{-}\cup B_{+}\cup B_{-}\cup C_{+}\cup C_{-}\subseteq L.
\]
Conversely, the discussion in Sections 1-3 shows that a point of L either has all three squares equal (giving the four lines) or, after possibly relabelling the coordinates, satisfies the hypotheses of Section 2, which place it on exactly one of the six ellipses.  Therefore
\[
 L=(\ell_{1}\cup\ell_{2}\cup\ell_{3}\cup\ell_{4})\cup(A_{+}\cup A_{-}\cup B_{+}\cup B_{-}\cup C_{+}\cup C_{-}).
\]

5.  Intersections.
The two ellipses in the same family meet in two points; for instance
\(A_{+}\cap A_{-}=\{(0,\pm\sqrt2,0)\}.\)  Intersections between ellipses from different families occur precisely along the four lines (2).  These intersection facts do not alter the classification of L as the union of four distinct lines and six distinct ellipses.

Hence the locus of the equation a(x)=a(y)=a(z) consists of exactly the ten sets listed above, completing the proof.

core_steps: Take pairwise differences of the equal expressions so they equal 0.
Factor each difference as (xÂ²âˆ’yÂ²)(xÂ²+yÂ²âˆ’1)=0 (difference of squares trick).
Split the locus according to which factor vanishes, defining four sets A,B,C,D.
Recognize A,B,C as cylinder-plane intersections (ellipses) and D as coordinate-equality lines.
Show the union AâˆªBâˆªCâˆªD equals the whole locus by double containment.
